Claude Code's local session JSONL isn't otherwise viewable live or remotely; this gives a browsable, shareable window into an agent's live activity.
give an LLM persistent state across chat sessions for a tutoring use case, without any app or database.

Running many autonomous LLM agents against untrusted input (email, web) on shared infrastructure creates real attack surface (memory poisoning, injection, SSRF, tool overreach) that generic agent frameworks don't address.
a shared, versioned kernel vocabulary (types + ports) is needed so multiple Agent OS runtime crates can interoperate instead of each defining ad-hoc session/tool/policy types
agent runtimes need typed, replayable session state and sandboxed tool execution rather than ad-hoc in-memory agent loops
Autonomous agent workflows drift without deterministic verification gates; this packages a reusable harness/checklist to keep agent-driven repos in a controlled feedback loop.
agent-driven browsers get blocked by anti-bot defenses, can't hand off to a human when stuck, and cross-contaminate sessions when run in parallel

Agent frameworks accumulate abstraction layers that constrain the model's action space; the team wants the thinnest possible loop that still solves premature-stop and context-blowup problems from browser/GUI agent work.
No apples-to-apples way to compare browser-automation agent frameworks and browser providers on real tasks.
existing browser-automation abstractions box the agent into rigid actions; sometimes the agent needs to write arbitrary CDP/JS code and remember what worked per-site
Websites aren't built for AI agents to navigate reliably; agents need a robust browser-automation layer with agent-friendly perception and action primitives.
Browser-automation frameworks add abstraction layers between the agent and the page; letting the agent write and rerun real CDP-driving code is faster and more general.
Users want a 24/7 agent that can browse the real web with persistent logins/cookies and be operated remotely via chat, without managing infra or babysitting login/2FA walls themselves.
Manually googling around to find how to reach a specific person is slow; let an agent do the open-web lookup.
agent frameworks like eve need real browser control but don't want to manage local Chrome or browser infra themselves

Comparing multiple browser-use agent variants (Mix, Manus, browser-use itself) needs a shared, reproducible evaluation harness across parallel cloud browser providers rather than ad hoc per-agent scripts.
distributing and discovering coding-agent plugins for browser automation across multiple agent hosts (Claude Code, Codex, Grok)
Users want to delegate browser tasks (form-filling, account actions, bookings) to an agent while retaining live visibility and control from a terminal.
vibe-coded websites ship with UI bugs, broken links, and a11y issues no one manually QAs.
LLMs can't watch video, and frame-dumping is prohibitively token-expensive; give the agent a structured, DOM-like textual read of a video instead.
running and observing a browser-use agent required writing Python scripts; a web UI makes it interactively usable and demoable

Web agents today either brittle-click UIs built for humans or rely on fragmented backend integrations (MCP/APIs); sites need a simple, code-first, self-declared way to expose callable functions to agents.
Pure LLM browser agents are slow, expensive, and non-deterministic for repeated tasks; pure RPA scripts break when the page changes and have no fallback.

give people a hands-on demo to explore what a computer-use agent controlling a real browser looks like, without building infrastructure themselves
LLM apps need standardized cloud browser sessions they can control via MCP without managing their own browser infrastructure.
raw CDP lacks custom commands/events, chrome.* extension API access, and stable cross-run element/page references, forcing every browser-automation tool to build its own harness.
shows developers a minimal working example of building a web agent instead of a black-box product like OpenAI Operator.
